content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration     •••     Executive/Legal     •••     Enforcement     •••     Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                          322-5501                    322-6441             322-6444

May 27, 1980


Jay L. Henke
Diversified Services to
Consumers
9776 Katella Avenue, Suite 109
Anaheim, CA 92804

Dear Mr. Henke:

     You have asked us to comment on the following factual
situation:

     Your advertising firm hopes to make money by securing
the permission of candidates to develop campaign posters
which endorse their candidacy and then marketing such
posters to merchants whose names will appear at the bottom
of the poster.

     You would underwrite all of the development, production
and distribution costs of the posters and would pay the
candidate a percentage of the sales price of each poster
sold to a merchant.

     The questions are: How would payments by a merchant
to you be reported, if at all? How would payments to the
candidate from you be reported, if at all? How would your
expenses in producing, distributing and posting the posters
be reported, if at all?

     The relevant portion of the Political Reform Act states
that:

     "Contribution" means a payment ... except to
the extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes. An expenditure made at the behest of a
candidate, committee or elected officer is a
contribution to the candidate, committee or
elected officer unless full and adequate considera-
tion is received for making the expenditure....

Government Code Section 82015
(Emphasis added.)

Jay L. Henke
Page Two                                    May 27, 1980


     In order to respond, a determination must be made as to
1) the "purposes" of the proposed transaction from the perspec-
tive of the candidate, the merchant and you; 2) whose "purposes"
or perspective should control; and 3) the effect of the mer-
chant receiving partial "consideration" for any payments he or
she might make.

Identification of Purposes

     It would appear that at least two of the parties to your
proposal (the candidate and the merchant) have at least some
political purpose for participating. And, while they may also
each have a "commercial purpose" such commercial purposes
appear subordinate to their political purposes.

     The candidate is receiving an opportunity both to raise
money (either for himself personally or for his campaign) and
to get campaign posters widely distributed. The latter
objective is clearly political, and the former may or may not
be political depending upon how the candidate intends to use
the money. It is important to note, however, that the candi-
date is eligible to participate in the plan solely because
of his "political" status as a candidate.1/

     In purchasing the posters, the merchant has mixed politi-
cal and commercial motives. Two of the merchant's purposes
are assisting the candidate by contributing money (albeit
indirectly) and by helping to advertise the candidate by means
of the posters. The latter is clearly political and the former
may be political. Although the merchant may also believe that
the posters will help him attract business, this "business"
purpose appears secondary to the merchant's political objec-
tives since it could be equally, if not better, accomplished
by having the candidate endorse the merchant's product or
establishment (not vice versa).


___________________________________

1/ A few candidates who also happen to be movie stars, TV
   anchormen, etc., may be able to command sizeable sums for
   endorsing products or establishments independent of their
   status as candidates. Payments received for such endorse-
   ments would probably be treated as income, not contributions.
   Here, however, it is not the candidate that is endorsing the
   product but vice versa. Consequently, the entire trans-
   action appears more politically than commercially oriented.

Jay L. Henke
Page Three                                May 27, 1980


     Your firm, like any other professional fundraiser or
seller of campaign materials is essentially in a venture to
make money. Any political purpose you may have is clearly
subordinate to its commercial one.

Whose Purpose Should Control

     The question of whose purpose--the maker or the receiver
of payments--controls under Section 82015 does not need to be
resolved in this specific instance since it appears that both
the ultimate donor of the funds (i.e., the merchant) and the
ultimate recipient of the funds (i.e., the candidate) both
have at least some (if not predominately) political purposes
for entering into the transaction. Certainly, under Section
82015, neither party could show clearly that the payments are
not being made for political purposes. Thus, all payments by
the merchant appear to be contributions.

     Of course, this approach ignores the existence of your
firm's purely commercial purposes. This seems appropriate,
however, because you are essentially acting as an agent of
the candidate for collecting donations and distributing
campaign posters.

Contribution Minus Consideration

     Since your firm is clearly receiving consideration (i.e.,
the use of the candidate's name and picture), none of your
payments to the candidate would be treated as contributions
from you. This is consistent with your role as an agent of
the candidate, or at the most a "go between" between the
merchant and the candidate.

     The merchant's situation is more complicated. As dis-
cussed above, the merchant is probably receiving some small
commercial consideration for buying the poster. Arriving at
what the value of such consideration is, however, would be
both extremely difficult and totally subjective. Further,
the Commission has expressly forbidden candidates from sub-
tracting the value of the dinner at a fundraising event in
reporting campaign contributions.

Method of Reporting (Contributions and Expenditures)

     Following the above analysis to its logical conclusion,
all payments made by the merchant to your firm would be
reported as contributions from the merchant to the candidate
with your firm as the intermediary.

Jay L. Henke
Page Four                                           May 27, 1980


     While there is some argument that the merchant's contribution should be broken down into two components--a monetary
contribution equal to the royalty payment made to the candidate,
and an in-kind contribution equivalent to the production and
distribution costs of the posters, we decline to adopt such an
approach for several reasons. First, it would be confusing to
both the candidate and the merchant and would involve excessive
paperwork. Second, under some arrangements for actually transferring funds from your firm to the candidate, it would be
almost impossible to utilize such a bifurcated system.
Finally, other than committees which must file campaign reports,
the Commission normally does not require in-kind contributors
to provide information concerning who actually receives their
expenditures, i.e., they inform the candidate that they have
contributed $1,000 in posters and are not required to state to
whom the $1,000 was paid. Thus, under circumstances where no
royalty payments are actually made, your identity might go
completely unreported.

     With regard to reporting expenditures, we believe that
all monies spent or retained by your firm for developing,
printing, merchandising and distributing the posters should be
reported by the candidate as expenditures. This is appropriate
even though the candidate "technically" has not paid any
money to your firm but rather has allowed you to "pre-spend"
money expected from contributions. This approach ensures that
your firm is treated like any other professional fundraising
firm2/ and that the contribution and expenditure ledgers in the
candidate's campaign statements balance.

     In summary, the candidate would report all payments to your
firm from the merchant as contributions from the merchant with
your firm as the intermediary and all funds retained or spent by
your firm as expenditures.

     If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
feel free to call on me at (916) 322-6441.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel
RMS:nc

_________________________________
2/  The only difference between your firm and other fundraising
firms is in the technical manner in which you receive compensation, i.e., the candidate does not receive the contributions
and then pay your firm a percentage; rather, the opposite is
true.

D
S
C

Diversified Services to Consumers
9276 KATELLA AVENUE • SUITE 109 • ANAHEIM, CALIFORNIA 92804 • 714/539-9544

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
1100 K St. 2nd Floor
Sacramento, California
Attn: Mr. Bob Stern

Dear Mr. Stern,

As a follow up to our conversation to day, I will try to enlighten you as
to the nature of what we are contemplating.

My company presently is involved in producing poster sponsorship programs
for the purpose of raising funds for non-profit organizations. This is
done through the sale of advertising space on posters extolling the merits
of the particular organization we are dealing with. The organization then
participates in revenues generated from the advertising sales, and it has
been most gratifying so far.

Recently, the thought crossed my mind that we could do the same thing with
political candidates as we are now doing for non-profit organzations, with
some minor changes in our revenue sharing format.

Our intention will be to purchase the marketing rights for the use of the
candidates likeness on our poster program. The price we will pay the candidate will be predicated upon the gross sales of advertising. In this type
of program we will undertake to print the posters and to distribute them
as well, and the costs of this will be considered in the percentage that
the candidate will receive.

It is our opinion that revenue generated by the candidate from the poster
program can only be considered as income to the candidate, and the amount
that the candidate earns will be directly influenced by his popularity
with our advertising clientele. We will be reporting all earnings paid
to candidates to the IRS as income from royalties, or commissions.

We have determined that this business approach is not illegal in any way,
however we are very concerned that the candidates who contract with us
will be properly reporting this income in accordance with regulations
of your office.

I personally do not see this income as anything other than ordinary income
to the candidate, but I want to be sure of my ground. Therefore I would
appreciate it greatly if your office would give me a written opinion as to
your position in the matter.

Incidentally, our program is open to all candidates without regard to the
political affiliation.

Your opinion will be of great assistance to me in determining our approach
to this concept, as it may apply to Federal candidates. For this reason I
would be appreciative of a speedy response from you.

Thank you, in advance for your assistance.                Jay L. Henke

                                                        Jay L. Henke, President

CAPITAL DEVELOPMENT & FUNDING • LOAN COUNSELING • INSURANCE • CONSUMER ADVOCACY
